input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A commercial building’s physical infrastructure, modelled with Building Information Modelling (BIM) and enriched to include sensors, actuators and controllers, is the subject of the Digital Twin under investigation in the TwinLight and BOREALIS research projects. The building’s lighting and control systems serve as the primary focus for demonstrating model‑driven deployment, real‑time data synchronization and optimization across the life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers multiple service types: (1) Monitoring – continuous acquisition and reporting of building state via real‑time data exchange; (2) Control – bidirectional command and control of physical devices through the twin; (3) Prediction – predictive analytics that forecast asset performance and system behavior; (4) Optimization – simulation‑driven scenario generation to reduce operational costs and improve energy efficiency; and (5) Visualization – immersive, interactive interfaces that provide stakeholders with real‑time visual feedback. Facility Managers interact primarily with monitoring, control and visualization services, while DT Engineers extend predictive and optimization capabilities with custom analytics plugins.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven deployment framework built on top of BIM. Devices and controllers are modeled in BIM and then extracted via Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) techniques into structured runtime artifacts. The framework incorporates real‑time data exchange mechanisms that continuously synchronize physical building states with the digital twin. An interactive visualization layer delivers immersive interfaces for users. The architecture is modular, featuring standardized extension points that allow DT Engineers to integrate custom services and analytics plugins. The platform is therefore grounded in BIM, MDE, continuous synchronization, immersive visualization, and modular extensibility.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Continuous real‑time bidirectional data exchange ensures immediate synchronization between the physical building and its digital twin; the framework specifies a runtime data exchange capability that supports low‑latency monitoring and control, although exact frequency or latency limits are not documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: A modular, role‑centric architecture where the DT Framework acts as a central hub; Building Control Engineers model devices and initiate deployment, Facility Managers monitor and control in real‑time, and DT Engineers extend the framework via service integration. Standardized extension points allow modular services and analytics to be added without full redeployment, enabling runtime configuration management and scalable orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system derives its computational models from enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM) data, where Building Control Engineers embed device definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers) directly within the BIM environment. An MDE‑based process validates the enriched BIM, converts it into structured representations, and applies configuration before publishing model artifacts to the middleware. The resulting models consist of geometric (BIM meshes), behavioral (TwinComponent and FeatureParser abstractions that define control logic and state updates), and data (Eclipse Ditto device registrations and connection descriptors). Eclipse Ditto provides policy‑based state management, enforcing units and value ranges for sensor data and pre‑execution checks for actuator commands, while Eclipse Hono handles at‑least‑once message delivery. The models are interrelated: the validation component consumes the enriched BIM, the conversion component emits Heti‑ready metadata, the configuration component attaches Ditto device definitions and Mix, and finally the SDKs (UE Engine, Hono, Ditto) consume these artifacts to enable bidirectional synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The primary physical actuators are lighting devices controlled by the LightTwinComponent, which exposes on/off, brightness, and colour‑temperature (Kelvin) attributes. Control commands are transmitted as Ditto messages via the DittoController, routed through the UnrealTwinController that brokers between UE5 components and physical devices. Each actuator is addressed by a unique DittoDevice identifier, and the TwinComponent implements pre‑execution validation to prevent unsafe configurations. While the documentation does not specify additional actuator types, the architecture allows extension for other device classes (e.g., HVAC) through FeatureParser abstractions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined within the BIM model as part of the device configuration and are exposed as Ditto devices. Sensor data is transmitted through Eclipse Hono with at‑least‑once delivery semantics. Data integrity is ensured by Ditto policy‑based validation that checks units and ranges before publishing. Synchronization occurs on a 1‑second heartbeat interval, which feeds sensor updates to both the Physical twin and the UE5 scene rendering. Exact sampling frequencies are not specified in the documents, but the 1‑second heartbeat underpins real‑time observation of state changes.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system evaluates fidelity using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, calculated as the product of timeliness, reliability, and availability. In a 12‑minute measurement window with a 1‑second heartbeat, the observed scores were 0.997 for timeliness (measuring lag between physical state change and twin update), 1.0 for reliability (no update loss), and 1.0 for availability, yielding an ODTE of 0.997. Validation is performed via Eclipse Ditto’s policy engine, which enforces value ranges and performs pre‑execution checks for commands. Fault tolerance mechanisms include Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, providing resilience against network failures and ensuring the twin remains an accurate, up‑to‑date representation of its physical counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical asset sensor data is streamed in real‑time to the digital twin using the framework’s real‑time data integration stack, which incorporates Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto. This flow enables continuous synchronization of building status between the physical construction site and the digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control signals originating from the digital twin are transmitted back to the physical building control system by the DT framework’s bidirectional communication channel. Commands issued by facility managers or automation scripts are delivered via the same Hono/Ditto integration layer, allowing real‑time actuation of building systems. Confirmation of execution is captured by feedback collected through the framework’s monitoring pipelines.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT framework is built atop Eclipse Hono’s messaging stack and Eclipse Ditto’s twin registry, both of which operate over IP‑based networks. Hono acts as a high‑throughput message broker that supports multiple transport protocols (e.g., MQTT, HTTP) and handles the routing of telemetry and command messages between physical devices and the digital core. Ditto provides a device‑centric data model and publishes twin updates via standard REST endpoints. Network reliability is achieved through Hono’s support for clustered brokers and failover paths, while security is enforced by the underlying transport layer (TLS encryption among the components) and the middleware’s access control mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment is governed by a model‑driven automation framework that reduces manual configuration and enables reproducible digital twin setups. The framework orchestrates service deployment based on a declarative model that specifies component dependencies and required runtime environments. It supports modular service composition, allowing the twin’s constituent services to be instantiated in the target environment—whether on‑premises, at the edge, or in cloud infrastructure—according to the project’s infrastructure constraints. High availability is achieved by automatically scaling or replicating services as defined by the deployment model.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle begins with the Building Control Engineer enriching a BIM model with sensors, actuators, and controller definitions. The enriched BIM is then exported via a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline that extracts a structured representation and automatically generates deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. These artifacts are deployed, establishing real‑time data exchanges between the physical building and its digital counterpart. During operation the Facility Manager monitors and controls the twin through an interactive visualization interface. Extensibility is supported by modular extension points that allow a DT Engineer to inject custom services, enabling the Twin to evolve as new functionality or analytics are added.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a model‑driven approach: device modelling in BIM, automatic transformation via MDE, and generation of deployable artifacts. The Building Control Engineer initiates deployment by defining control logic within the BIM context. The DT Engineer extends functionality by developing and integrating custom services through the system’s standardized extension points. Quality assurance is implicit in the automated extraction and deployment pipeline, while evolution is managed by iteratively updating the enriched BIM and redeploying updated artifacts via the modular architecture.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The framework provides real‑time data exchange between physical and digital domains, enabling continuous synchronization. Interactive visualization offers immersive user interfaces for monitoring and controlling building operations. Custom services integrated through the modular architecture allow for analytics and processing capabilities, facilitating decision support, though specific confidence metrics or decision processes are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adopts BIM as the industry’s de‑facto standard for asset representation, leveraging BIM’s semantic richness for device, sensor, and control logic modeling. Extension points conform to a standardized interface definition, allowing plug‑in of external models or services while preserving interoperability. The approach directly addresses the noted lack of standardized integration frameworks in current AECO deployments and supports interoperability with existing open standards, although explicit certification or compliance frameworks are not yet defined.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

It captures the enrichment of BIM with sensors and actuators. However, the source text refers to the case study specifically as a 'Living Lab' rather than explicitly a 'commercial building', though the AECO context is accurate. Supported by quotes: 'We demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study' and 'TwinLight addresses... control semantics for lighting systems'.

The extraction accurately details the service types (monitoring, control, optimization, visualization) and aligns user roles perfectly with the source text. The text explicitly states that the 'Facility Manager Monitors system states... and operates the physical infrastructure' and the 'DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services'. It also lists 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and... optimization' as core capabilities.

The extraction is highly specific and grounded in the abstract and introduction. It correctly identifies the 'model-driven framework... grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', the specific tools 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'Unreal Engine' for visualization, and 'standardized extension points' for modularity.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately synthesizes the temporal behavior described in the text, highlighting the continuous and real-time nature of the data exchange. It correctly identifies the lack of specific numeric latency values in the source. Supported by quotes: 'real-time data exchange', 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', 'continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The source documents describe the roles of human actors (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers) and the modeling of a single DT instance for a Living Lab. They do not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures for twins, or coordination mechanisms between twins. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is correct. No relevant evidence is present regarding multiplicities of twins.

The description provides a comprehensive and accurate summary of the system's orchestration and architecture. It correctly identifies the framework as a 'central hub', details the specific roles from Table I, and describes the modular architecture with extension points and runtime configuration. Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'Building Control Engineer... initiates DT deployment', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability'.

The text mentions 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' and the integration of specific technologies (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal) as components of the framework's internal solution. However, it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems or specific integration patterns with other Digital Twins, as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct. No relevant evidence is present regarding horizontal integration with external enterprise systems or other twins.

TABLE VI: Deployment Times for Digital Twin


**Stage** **Duration** (seconds)


Model Checking 5.53
Model Parsing 8.7
Initialization and Setup 0.3


in 0.3 seconds. This considerably short deployment time of
14.53 seconds underscores the efficiency of our proposal in
streamlining the DT creation process. Our deployment was
done on a standard

The Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) [18] metric is a crucial measure of the synchronization quality between DTs and their physical counterparts. It is composed of
three main components: timeliness, reliability, and availability.
_Timeliness_ ( _T_ ) reflects how up-to-date the data is that the
DT receives, measuring the delay between a state change
occurring in the physical counterpart and the subsequent
update in the DT. _Reliability_ ( _R_ ) measures the consistency
in communication between the physical counterpart and the
DT, defined as the ratio of the number of state updates
successfully received by the DT to the number of updates
expected. _Availability_ ( _A_ ) assesses the operational uptime of
the physical counterpart as perceived by the DT, measuring
the extent to which the physical counterpart is accessible and
capable of sending updates. The ODTE score then is calculated
as the product of these components (cf. _ODTE_ = _T ×R×A_ ),
providing a comprehensive assessment of system performance.
Table VII shows our results.

In our assessment, the scores were 0.997 for timeliness
(indicating some slight delay in communication), 1.0 for
reliability (no message loss), and 1.0 for availability (100%
up-time) resulting in an overall ODTE value of 0.997. We
measured the ODTE metric over a continuous 12-minute



TABLE VII: ODTE Measurements


**ODTE Component** **Score**


Timeliness 0.997
Reliability 1.0
Availability 1.0


**Total Score** 0.997


measurement window. The setup employed a regular 1-second
heartbeat mechanism to coordinate data exchange and state
updates between the physical and digital twin. Throughout
the experiment, the DT maintained a consistent and exact
reflection of the physical system’s state, with no observable
divergence in state, reflecting ideal entanglement under the
given conditions. Our experiments assess bidirectional synchronization by transmitting lighting control commands from
the DT to the physical system and verifying the corresponding
state updates in the reverse direction. The absence of such
updates would indicate a communication failure, resulting in
a total ODTE score of 0, thereby validating the integrity of
the bidirectional communication channel.

are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

It correctly identifies the initiation by the Building Control Engineer, the MDE pipeline for generation, deployment, and the operation/maintenance phase involving the Facility Manager and DT Engineer. Quotes: 'Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates DT deployment from BIM models.', 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.', 'Facility Managers then interact with the deployed DT for real-time monitoring and control.', 'DT Engineers extend the framework’s capabilities through service integration.'

The extraction correctly describes the model-driven approach, the roles of engineers in the process, and the modular architecture for evolution. It accurately reflects the focus on automated transformation and deployment. However, it could be improved by explicitly mentioning the validation step mentioned in the text. Quotes: 'model-driven deployment framework that leverages Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a semantic backbone for automating the creation, configuration, and deployment of DTs', 'Our approach systematically extracts relevant asset data from a BIM model and generates runtime interfaces for bidirectional communication.', 'standardized extension points for modular service integration, supporting scalable deployment and future extensibility.'

The extraction is highly specific and well-grounded in the source text. It accurately captures the real-time synchronization, interactive visualization capabilities (Unreal Engine), and the role of custom services for analytics. It correctly notes the absence of specific confidence metrics. Quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations', 'Unreal Engine 5 [17] provides a rich visualization environment for interactive monitoring and simulation.', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms.'

The extraction accurately reflects the paper's stance on using BIM as the industry standard to address the lack of standardized integration frameworks. It correctly identifies the solution's reliance on standardized extension points and open standards like Eclipse Ditto/Hono.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
